problem_statement.Title: --notes option ignores punctuation-only note tags (e.g. "???")
problem_statement.Description: When a note tag passed via --notes is composed entirely of punctuation characters (for example "???"), EncodingChecker's regex does not match it and pylint does not emit the W0511 (fixme) warning for that comment. Word-character tags (e.g. "YES") are matched correctly.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) In EncodingChecker (pylint/checkers/misc.py, method: open), the fixme detection regex is constructed with a trailing word-boundary '\b':
   - code builds notes alternation as: notes = "|".join(re.escape(note) for note in self.config.notes)
   - then uses: regex_string = rf"#\s*({notes}|{self.config.notes_rgx})\b" or rf"#\s*({notes})\b"
   - This encodes the assumption that note tags end in a word character. A '\b' requires a boundary between a word (\w) and a non-word (\W); when a tag is composed entirely of non-word characters (punctuation) and the character after the tag is also non-word (e.g. '???:'), the '\b' does not match and the tag is not detected. The correct relationship for the trailing boundary is to require that the following character is NOT a word character—e.g. use a negative lookahead '(?!\w)'—so both word-character tags and punctuation-only tags are matched appropriately.

2) Related parsing assumption in pragma parsing (pylint/utils/pragma_parser.py):
   - parse_pragma's MESSAGE_STRING token is defined as r"[0-9A-Za-z\-\_]{2,}", which only recognizes message identifiers composed of letters/digits/hyphen/underscore and at least two characters. This means inline pragmas such as "# pylint: disable=???" will not parse "???" as a message identifier. That is a separate, related assumption about allowed characters in identifiers: it does not cause the missing W0511 in normal comments (the regex in EncodingChecker is the direct cause), but it means that even after fixing the regex, punctuation-only notes cannot be disabled via inline pragmas because parse_pragma will not recognize them. Both of these are distinct code locations that encode assumptions about allowable characters for note/message identifiers and therefore should be considered together when reasoning about notes that use punctuation-only tokens.
StepsToReproduce: Create test.py with two comment lines:
# YES: yes
Run: pylint test.py --notes="YES,???"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior:
Pylint reports a W0511 (fixme) warning for both comment lines when both tags are included via --notes. Example output should include both:
************* Module test
test.py:1:1: W0511: YES: yes (fixme)
test.py:2:1: W0511: ???: no (fixme)
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pylint reports a W0511 (fixme) warning only for the word-character tag line (e.g. "YES") but not for the punctuation-only tag (e.g. "???"). Example output shows only the first warning and omits the second.

hints_text: Did a little investigation, this is we're actually converting this option in a regular expression pattern (thereby making it awfully similar to the `notes-rgx` option). Since `?` is a special character in regex this doesn't get picked up. Using `\?\?\?` in either `notes` or `notes-rgx` should work.

problem_statement.Title: Console traceback printed when running pylint with an unrecognized CLI option (e.g. pylint -Q)
problem_statement.Description: When an unknown command-line option is passed to the pylint console script, pylint prints the linter's "Unrecognized option" message but then a full Python traceback is emitted (ending with pylint.config.exceptions._UnrecognizedOptionError). This occurs when invoking pylint from the command line (console entrypoint).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Top-level entrypoint does not handle the specific user-facing exception — pylint/__init__.py:run_pylint calls PylintRun(argv) inside a try/except that only catches KeyboardInterrupt and therefore does not intercept pylint.config.exceptions._UnrecognizedOptionError. Because the exception is unhandled at the console entrypoint, it propagates to Python's default handler and a full traceback is printed to the user (see pylint/__init__.py lines 24–27).

2) Configuration parsing emits a friendly message then raises an exception for programmatic callers — there are two related raising sites that follow the same pattern and must be considered together with (1):
   - pylint/config/config_initialization.py::_config_initialization: when unrecognized options are found in the parsed command line the function calls linter.add_message("unrecognized-option", ...) and then raises _UnrecognizedOptionError(options=unrecognized_options) (see config_initialization.py lines 82–85). The config-file parsing path also converts an arguments-manager-raised _UnrecognizedOptionError into linter.add_message (lines 55–60) before continuing.
   - pylint/config/arguments_manager.py::_parse_configuration_file: when unknown options are discovered in a configuration file this method raises _UnrecognizedOptionError(options=unrecognized_options) (see arguments_manager.py lines ~240–250). config_initialization.py catches that and turns it into a linter.add_message in the config-file path (see config_initialization.py lines 55–60).

3) Interaction that produces the observable output: Run.__init__ (pylint/lint/run.py) calls _config_initialization (run.py lines 135–137). If _config_initialization raises _UnrecognizedOptionError (after having emitted the human-friendly message via the reporter), that exception is not caught by Run.__init__ nor by the top-level run_pylint wrapper, so the friendly message is printed and then the Python traceback for the unhandled _UnrecognizedOptionError is shown. This propagation behavior is intentional for programmatic Run callers (tests expect Run to raise), but it results in a traceback when the code path is exercised via the console entrypoint.
StepsToReproduce: Open a shell/terminal where pylint is installed.
Run: pylint -Q
Observe stdout/stderr.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When an unrecognized CLI option is supplied, pylint prints a concise, user-friendly error message about the unrecognized option and exits with a non-zero usage/configuration exit code (consistent with the project's conventions) without printing a Python traceback.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: pylint prints the human-friendly message (for example: "Command line:1:0: E0015: Unrecognized option found: Q (unrecognized-option)") and then a full Python traceback is printed to stderr, terminating with pylint.config.exceptions._UnrecognizedOptionError.

problem_statement.Title: ignore-paths not applied when running with --recursive=y (package expansion skips configured ignore-paths)
problem_statement.Description: When running pylint with --recursive=y on a package, patterns set in ignore-paths (pyproject.toml / [tool.pylint.MASTER]) are not applied and files under matching paths (e.g. src/gen/*) are still linted.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) pylint.lint.pylinter.PyLinter._expand_files (pylint/lint/pylinter.py) calls expand_modules(...) and passes the attribute self._ignore_paths as the ignore-paths argument: expand_modules(modules, self.config.ignore, self.config.ignore_patterns, self._ignore_paths). (See pylinter.py: ~772-779.) 2) The attribute self._ignore_paths is initialized inside PyLinter.open() by assigning self._ignore_paths = self.linter.config.ignore_paths (pylinter.py: ~1003-1015). The code therefore relies on a cached attribute (_ignore_paths) rather than consistently reading the authoritative configuration namespace (self.config.ignore_paths). 3) Elsewhere the code uses the authoritative namespace: pylint.lint.pylinter.PyLinter._discover_files uses self.config.ignore_paths when skipping subtrees during recursive discovery (pylinter.py: ~600-605). This makes the use of the cached attribute in _expand_files an inconsistent pattern. 4) pylint.lint.expand_modules.expand_modules expects the ignore-paths argument (ignore_list_paths_re) and will skip subfiles only if that argument contains the compiled patterns; when a stale or empty value is passed, expand_modules cannot apply the user's ignore-paths and will return subfiles that should have been ignored (expand_modules.py: ~76-81, ~137-146). In short: the defect is an inconsistency where package expansion is fed a cached/stale attribute (self._ignore_paths) rather than the authoritative current configuration (self.config.ignore_paths), so expand_modules may be called with an empty or out-of-date ignore-paths list and thus fail to exclude paths the user configured to ignore.
StepsToReproduce: Create pyproject.toml with [tool.pylint.MASTER] ignore-paths = ["^src/gen/.*$"]
Run: pylint --recursive=y src/
Observe that files under src/gen/ are reported by pylint (despite matching the ignore-paths pattern)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When running pylint with --recursive=y, files and directories that match ignore-paths (e.g. src/gen/*) are excluded from linting and no messages are reported for those files.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pylint reports messages for files under ignored paths (src/gen/*) even though ignore-paths contains a pattern intended to skip them.

Hmm I can reproduce your error, and now I understand the root cause. The root cause is following. The decision of skipping the path is here:

https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/3c5eca2ded3dd2b59ebaf23eb289453b5d2930f0/pylint/lint/pylinter.py#L600-L607

* When you execute pylint with `src/` argument following variables are present:
```python
(Pdb) p root
'src/gen'
(Pdb) p self.config.ignore_paths
[re.compile('^src\\\\gen\\\\.*$|^src/gen/.*$')]
```

* When you uexecute pylint with `.` argument following variables are present:
```python
(Pdb) p root
'./src/gen'
(Pdb) p self.config.ignore_paths
[re.compile('^src\\\\gen\\\\.*$|^src/gen/.*$')]
```

In the second case, the source is prefixed with `./` which causes that path is not matched. The simple fix should be to use  `os.path.normpath()` https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.normpath

patch:
diff --git a/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py b/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
--- a/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
+++ b/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
@@ -82,8 +82,10 @@ def expand_modules(
             continue
         module_path = get_python_path(something)
         additional_search_path = [".", module_path] + path
-        if os.path.exists(something):
-            # this is a file or a directory
+        if os.path.isfile(something) or os.path.exists(
+            os.path.join(something, "__init__.py")
+        ):
+            # this is a file or a directory with an explicit __init__.py
             try:
                 modname = ".".join(
                     modutils.modpath_from_file(something, path=additional_search_path)
@@ -103,9 +105,7 @@ def expand_modules(
                 )
                 if filepath is None:
                     continue
-            except (ImportError, SyntaxError) as ex:
-                # The SyntaxError is a Python bug and should be
-                # removed once we move away from imp.find_module: https://bugs.python.org/issue10588
+            except ImportError as ex:
                 errors.append({"key": "fatal", "mod": modname, "ex": ex})
                 continue
         filepath = os.path.normpath(filepath)

problem_statement.Title: pylint fails with F0010 parse-error when a directory contains a module with the same name (tries to parse non-existent __init__.py)
problem_statement.Description: When invoking pylint on a directory (for example `pylint a`) where the directory contains a file with the same basename as the directory (e.g. a/a.py) but the directory has no __init__.py, pylint attempts to parse a/__init__.py (which does not exist) and reports a parse-error (F0010). Renaming the inner file (so it no longer equals the directory name) or adding an __init__.py avoids the failure.
problem_statement.RootCause:
All of the following incorrect assumptions and encodings in code cause the observed parse-error; each item below is an independent but related contributor and they have equal weight:

1) expand_modules constructs a package filepath for any directory argument by setting filepath = os.path.join(something, "__init__.py") whenever os.path.isdir(something) is True. This assignment is performed without checking whether that __init__.py actually exists; the code unconditionally uses the literal "__init__.py" as the filepath for directories (pylint/lint/expand_modules.py). As a result, expand_modules can produce a ModuleDescription whose "path" points to a non-existent file (e.g., a/__init__.py).

2) The test used to decide whether an entry represents a package-init is based on the basename of filepath (os.path.basename(filepath) == "__init__.py") and does not require that the file exists. In expand_modules the has_init expression relies on the basename check alone, so a constructed filepath whose basename is "__init__.py" is treated as if the package init file were present even when the file is absent (pylint/lint/expand_modules.py).

3) expand_modules appends a ModuleDescription entry using the computed filepath unconditionally when the module is not detected as a namespace (the result.append uses that filepath). Because filepath can be the non-existent a/__init__.py (see items 1–2), the linter later attempts to parse that non-existent path (pylint/lint/expand_modules.py). The append does not verify filesystem existence before returning the descriptor to the caller.

4) expand_modules falls back to a simple basename-derived module name when astroid.modutils.modpath_from_file(...) raises ImportError: modname = os.path.splitext(basename)[0]. That fallback produces names like "a" for an input path 'a'. Combined with (1) and (2), the fallback increases the likelihood that a directory argument will be represented as a package with filepath 'a/__init__.py' even when no such file exists (pylint/lint/expand_modules.py lines controlling modname fallback and later handling of spec).

5) The downstream caller consumes the ModuleDescription entries returned by expand_modules and attempts to build an AST from the supplied filepath and module name. PyLinter.get_ast calls astroid.MANAGER.ast_from_file(filepath, modname, source=True); when the provided filepath points to a non-existent a/__init__.py the astroid call raises a building error, and PyLinter reports this as the observed F0010 parse-error (pylint/lint/pylinter.py). In short: expand_modules can hand PyLinter a "path" that does not exist and PyLinter attempts to parse that path, producing the observed error message.

Contextual note: pylinter._discover_files contains logic that treats directories that lack __init__.py differently (it explicitly checks os.path.isfile(os.path.join(something, "__init__.py")) when walking directories). The failing case occurs when expand_modules is used (non-recursive invocation) and expand_modules does not perform the similar existence guard, so expand_modules is the locus of the defect for the non-recursive path.
StepsToReproduce: Create directory a containing two empty files a.py and b.py (no __init__.py):
mkdir a && printf "" > a/a.py && printf "" > a/b.py
Run: pylint a
Observe the parse-error for a/__init__.py. Alternatively, rename a/a.py to a/c.py or create a/__init__.py and re-run to see the error disappear.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Running `pylint a` when 'a' is a directory with module files (even if the directory has no __init__.py) should succeed and lint the actual module files inside the directory. Pylint should not attempt to parse a non-existent a/__init__.py and should handle directories without __init__.py (namespace/no-init directories) by scanning their actual module files rather than producing descriptors that point to missing __init__.py files.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pylint attempts to parse a/__init__.py and emits a parse-error (F0010: Unable to load file a/__init__.py: [Errno 2] No such file or directory) when the directory contains a file with the same name as the directory and no __init__.py exists. Renaming the inner file or creating __init__.py makes the error go away.

diff --git a/pylint/config/argument.py b/pylint/config/argument.py
--- a/pylint/config/argument.py
+++ b/pylint/config/argument.py
@@ -99,11 +99,20 @@ def _py_version_transformer(value: str) -> tuple[int, ...]:
     return version
 
 
+def _regex_transformer(value: str) -> Pattern[str]:
+    """Return `re.compile(value)`."""
+    try:
+        return re.compile(value)
+    except re.error as e:
+        msg = f"Error in provided regular expression: {value} beginning at index {e.pos}: {e.msg}"
+        raise argparse.ArgumentTypeError(msg)
+
+
 def _regexp_csv_transfomer(value: str) -> Sequence[Pattern[str]]:
     """Transforms a comma separated list of regular expressions."""
     patterns: list[Pattern[str]] = []
     for pattern in _csv_transformer(value):
-        patterns.append(re.compile(pattern))
+        patterns.append(_regex_transformer(pattern))
     return patterns
 
 
@@ -130,7 +139,7 @@ def _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer(value: str) -> Sequence[Pattern[str]]:
     "non_empty_string": _non_empty_string_transformer,
     "path": _path_transformer,
     "py_version": _py_version_transformer,
-    "regexp": re.compile,
+    "regexp": _regex_transformer,
     "regexp_csv": _regexp_csv_transfomer,
     "regexp_paths_csv": _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer,
     "string": pylint_utils._unquote,

problem_statement.Title: Configuration regex with \p{Han} (\p Unicode property) raises re.error during config parsing
problem_statement.Description: When a Pylint option that accepts a regular expression (for example function-rgx / regexp types) contains a Perl-style Unicode property escape such as "\p{Han}", Pylint crashes during configuration/argument parsing with re.error: bad escape \p. The failure happens because Pylint compiles user-supplied regex option values using Python's builtin re module and, in some places, assumes compiled regex objects are instances of re.Pattern.
problem_statement.RootCause:
Multiple places in the codebase assume user-provided regular-expression option values are compilable by Python's builtin re and/or that compiled patterns are instances of re.Pattern. These incorrect assumptions are encoded in the following locations (each listed with the problematic behavior observed):

- pylint/config/option.py:
  - _regexp_validator calls re.compile(value) when the option value is not already a compiled pattern (file lines 23–26). This compiles config regexes using the builtin re engine.
  - _regexp_csv_validator delegates to _regexp_validator and thereby compiles CSV regex entries (file lines 30–31).
  - _regexp_paths_csv_validator builds a composed pattern for path matching and calls re.compile(...) on it (file lines 34–48).
  - VALIDATORS['regexp'] is set to a lambda that calls re.compile(pattern or "") (file line 106), so option-parsing/validation flows will call builtin re.compile directly.

- pylint/config/argument.py:
  - _TYPE_TRANSFORMERS maps 'regexp' to re.compile (file line 133), i.e. argparse will call re.compile as the type transformer.
  - _regexp_csv_transfomer and _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer call re.compile(...) for each CSV entry (file lines 102–107 and 110–121).
  These uses mean argparse parsing/transforming of option values will run re.compile on user-supplied strings and can raise re.error for constructs unsupported by re (for example \p{...}).

- pylint/config/utils.py:
  - _parse_rich_type_value uses isinstance(value, re.Pattern) to detect compiled patterns and return value.pattern when serializing (file lines 149–156). This check does not recognise compiled patterns created by third-party regex engines (their compiled type is not re.Pattern), so serialization/setting-of-options can mishandle such compiled objects.

- pylint/config/arguments_manager.py:
  - TOML generation checks isinstance(value, re.Pattern) and the list-case similarly (file lines 735–741). This means the TOML/serialization path only recognises re.Pattern and will not correctly serialize compiled patterns from other regex engines.
  - set_option uses _parse_rich_type_value (which performs the isinstance(re.Pattern) check noted above) when parsing programmatic option setting.

- doc/exts/pylint_options.py:
  - The docs/options example generator similarly uses isinstance(value, re.Pattern) (file lines 99–107) and so assumes compiled patterns are re.Pattern for producing example TOML entries.

- pylint/checkers/base/name_checker/checker.py:
  - NameChecker.open unconditionally calls re.compile(rgxp) for values in self.linter.config.good_names_rgxs and self.linter.config.bad_names_rgxs (file lines 285–290). This re-compilation step assumes config entries are strings or re.Pattern-compatible objects and does not tolerate compiled pattern objects from other regex engines.

Net effect of these assumptions:
- If a user-supplied regex uses syntax not supported by Python's builtin re (for example \p{Han}), re.compile will raise re.error during argument/config parsing (either in argparse's type conversion or in option validators), producing a traceback and causing Pylint to crash on startup instead of surfacing a clearer user-facing diagnostic.
- If compiled regex objects originate from a non-re engine (for example the third-party 'regex' module), code that uses isinstance(value, re.Pattern) or that calls re.compile on already-compiled objects may not recognise or may mishandle those compiled objects.

Correctly-stated relationship (diagnostic):
- Configuration parsing and serialization need to treat regex option values as textual patterns that may require regex engines beyond the builtin re. In addition, code that inspects or serializes compiled patterns must recognise compiled pattern objects by their runtime behavior/attributes (e.g., presence of a .pattern attribute and matching methods) rather than assuming they are instances of re.Pattern. Under this diagnosis, user-visible behavior should either accept patterns that require other engines when those engines are available, or fail the option parse with a clear user-facing error that explains the unsupported construct.
StepsToReproduce: Create a .pylintrc containing a regex option that uses a Unicode property escape, for example:
function-rgx=[\p{Han}a-z_][\p{Han}a-z0-9_]{2,30}$
Run pylint in a project that loads that pylintrc (for example, run `pylint` from the repo root).
Observe that Pylint crashes during startup with a traceback ending in: re.error: bad escape \p at position 1.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pylint should not crash while parsing the configuration. For config/CLI regex options that use constructs unsupported by Python's builtin re (for example \p{...}), Pylint should surface a clear, user-facing diagnostic rather than an uncaught re.error traceback. If an external regex engine is required for certain constructs, the configuration parsing/validation and serialization should handle either the textual pattern or a compiled-pattern object in a way that does not rely on the compiled object being exactly re.Pattern.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pylint raises an uncaught re.error during config parsing (the traceback culminates in re.error: bad escape \p at position 1) when a config regex contains \p{Han}. The exception originates in direct calls to re.compile used by argparse-type transformers and option validators.

hints_text:
This doesn't seem like it is a `pylint` issue?

`re.compile("[\p{Han}a-z_]")` also raises normally. `\p` also isn't documented: https://docs.python.org/3/howto/regex.html
Is this a supported character?
I think this could be improved! Similar to the helpful output when passing an unrecognized option to Pylint, we could give a friendly output indicating that the regex pattern is invalid without the traceback; happy to put a MR together if you agree.
Thanks @mbyrnepr2 I did not even realize it was a crash that we had to fix before your comment.
@mbyrnepr2 I think in the above stacktrace on line 1858 makes the most sense.

We need to decide though if we continue to run the program. I think it makes sense to still quit. If we catch regex errors there and pass we will also "allow" ignore path regexes that don't work. I don't think we should do that.

Imo, incorrect regexes are a little different from other "incorrect" options, since there is little risk that they are valid on other interpreters or versions such as old messages etc. Therefore, I'd prefer to (cleanly) exit.
Indeed @DanielNoord I think we are on the same page regarding this point; I would also exit instead of passing if the regex is invalid. That line you mention, we can basically try/except on re.error and exit printing the details of the pattern which is invalid.

patch:
diff --git a/pylint/reporters/text.py b/pylint/reporters/text.py
--- a/pylint/reporters/text.py
+++ b/pylint/reporters/text.py
@@ -175,7 +175,7 @@ def on_set_current_module(self, module: str, filepath: str | None) -> None:
         self._template = template
 
         # Check to see if all parameters in the template are attributes of the Message
-        arguments = re.findall(r"\{(.+?)(:.*)?\}", template)
+        arguments = re.findall(r"\{(\w+?)(:.*)?\}", template)
         for argument in arguments:
             if argument[0] not in MESSAGE_FIELDS:
                 warnings.warn(

     """TODO remove in 3.0."""

problem_statement.Title: TextReporter mis-parses --msg-template with escaped braces (e.g. '{{ "Category": "{category}" }}') causing warnings and broken output
problem_statement.Description: When using a message template that includes literal braces via format-string escaping ({{ and }}), Pylint's text reporter emits a UserWarning about unrecognized template arguments and prints corrupted fragments instead of the intended rendered dictionary. Example: using --msg-template='{{ "Category": "{category}" }}' on Pylint 2.12.2 / Python 3.9 produces warnings about an argument containing the literal text and yields lines like '" }' rather than '{ "Category": "convention" }'.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The template-validation logic in TextReporter.on_set_current_module (pylint/reporters/text.py) uses a naive regex to discover format fields: re.findall(r"\{(.+?)(:.*)?\}", template). This pattern matches any content between single braces and does not implement Python new-style format-string parsing semantics (it does not account for escaped literal braces '{{' and '}}'). As a result, literal text inside escaped braces can be incorrectly identified as a format field.

2) The code removes unrecognized tokens by constructing a regex from the matched argument without escaping metacharacters: template = re.sub(r"\{" + argument[0] + r"(:.*?)?\}", "", template). Because argument[0] is inserted raw into a regex, metacharacters in the matched text (for example '.' or '[') can be interpreted as regex operators, and the removal may strip literal template content erroneously.

3) There is a semantic mismatch between the reporter's field-discovery logic and the formatter used at output time: Message.format (pylint/message/message.py) applies template.format(**asdict(self)) which follows Python's format-string rules (including that '{{' and '}}' render as literal braces). The regex-based discovery does not follow those same rules, so it can identify tokens that str.format would not treat as fields, producing spurious warnings and template corruption.

4) Impact/scope: Because TextReporter.on_set_current_module implements the validation and the other text reporters inherit this behavior and are registered from the same module, the issue affects all text-based reporters that use this code path (TextReporter and its subclasses such as ParseableTextReporter, VSTextReporter, and ColorizedTextReporter). MESSAGE_FIELDS (computed from fields(Message) in pylint/reporters/text.py) is the authoritative set used to decide which fields are recognized; the incorrect regex and unsafe removal are the root causes of mis-identifying literal escaped content as unknown fields.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Python file that triggers at least one pylint message (example filename: test.py).
Run: pylint test.py --msg-template='{{ "Category": "{category}" }}'
Observe the UserWarning about an unrecognized argument and that the output lines are corrupted rather than showing the dictionary with "Category" as the key.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Each message is printed using the supplied template with literal braces preserved; for the example template the output lines should look like: { "Category": "convention" } (i.e., the dictionary with "Category" as the key and the message category as the value). No UserWarning about an unrecognized argument should be emitted for literal text contained in escaped braces.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A UserWarning is emitted: "Don't recognize the argument '{ "Category' in the --msg-template..." (or similar, depending on how the regex captures the content) and the printed messages are corrupted, showing only fragments such as '" }' rather than the intended dictionary output.

hints_text:
Subsequently, there is also this behavior with the quotes
```
$ pylint test.py --msg-template='"Category": "{category}"'
************* Module test
Category": "convention
Category": "error
Category": "error
Category": "convention
Category": "convention
Category": "error

$ pylint test.py --msg-template='""Category": "{category}""'
************* Module test
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "error"
"Category": "error"
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "error"
```
Commit that changed the behavior was probably this one: https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/commit/7c3533ca48e69394391945de1563ef7f639cd27d#diff-76025f0bc82e83cb406321006fbca12c61a10821834a3164620fc17c978f9b7e

And I tested on 2.11.1 that it is working as intended on that version.
Thanks for digging into this !
